title: Suing Their Own Boss: Emory Professors Take University to Court Over Protest Arrests
summary:
Imagine you're a teacher at your school, and one day you see students peacefully setting up tents on the lawn to protest something they believe in. You decide to just watch—maybe even help someone who looks confused. Then, suddenly, police swarm in and arrest you. That's exactly what three professors at Emory University in Atlanta say happened to them—and now they're suing their own employer.

The lawsuit, filed Thursday in DeKalb County State Court, involves Noelle McAfee (philosophy), Emilio Del Valle-Escalante (English and Indigenous studies), and Caroline Fohlin (economics). All three are tenured professors (meaning they have job security and can't be easily fired). None of them were convicted of any crime. Yet they were arrested on April 25, 2024, during a pro-Palestine protest on campus.

Here's what each professor says happened:

McAfee says she yelled "Stop!" at an officer who was roughly arresting a protester. She was charged with disorderly conduct. Del Valle-Escalante says he was trying to help an elderly woman when he was arrested on the same charge. Fohlin says she protested when officers pinned a protester to the ground—and was thrown face-first to the ground herself, suffering a concussion (a brain injury) and a spine injury. She was charged with misdemeanor battery of an officer.

The professors argue that Emory broke its own policies by calling in Atlanta police and Georgia state troopers without first trying alternative ways to handle the protest. They say the university's open expression policy was violated.

"The judicial system would find that Emory failed to protect its students, to protect its staff, to protect the educational mission of the university," McAfee said. She served as president of the Emory University Senate after her arrest and helped draft the school's open expression policy.

The university disagrees. Spokesperson Laura Diamond said the lawsuit is "without merit" and that Emory "acts appropriately and responsibly to keep our community safe from threats of harm."

This case is part of a larger national debate. Since the 2023-2024 protests over the Gaza war, many students and faculty have sued universities, claiming their free speech was suppressed. Advocates say there's a "Palestine exception" where schools are willing to limit pro-Palestine speech. Palestine Legal, a legal aid group, says it received 300 percent more requests for help in 2025 than before 2023.

The professors want the university to pay back the money they spent defending themselves and to change its policies. "I'm suing to try to get them to be accountable and to change," McAfee said.
why_it_matters: This case highlights ongoing tensions between campus free speech and safety, and how legal systems are being used to challenge university policies regarding protest.

keywords.explanation: Faculty members at a university, in this case tenured professors with job security.
Emory University in Atlanta, Georgia, the institution being sued.
Individuals enrolled at the university who participated in the protest.
A public demonstration expressing opposition, here against the Gaza war.
The act of police taking individuals into custody, here during a protest.
A legal action brought to court seeking remedy, in this case for alleged policy violations.

background_read: The Gaza war protests on U.S. college campuses in 2023-2024 were part of a long history of student activism.
Since the 1960s, students have protested wars and social issues.
Many universities have faced lawsuits over free speech.
The term 'Palestine exception' refers to critics' claim that schools treat pro-Palestine activism more harshly than other forms of protest.
Palestine Legal is an organization that provides legal support to those advocating for Palestinian rights.
Article_Structure: LEAD: Analogy of a teacher arrested while watching students protest
└─ specific: 'Three professors at Emory University...are suing their own employer'
KEY PARTIES: Three tenured professors: McAfee, Del Valle-Escalante, Fohlin
├─ WHAT: Students set up tents pro-Palestine
└─ ARRESTS: Professors say they were just watching or helping
├─ Del Valle-Escalante: helping elderly → disorderly conduct
└─ Fohlin: protested pinning → concussion, spine injury, battery charge
LEGAL ACTION: Lawsuit in DeKalb County State Court
├─ Allegation: Emory broke own open expression policy
└─ Demand: repayment of legal fees and policy change
UNIVERSITY RESPONSE: Spokesperson says lawsuit 'without merit'
NATIONAL CONTEXT: Palestine exception, 300% increase in legal aid requests
└─ Quote from McAfee: seeking accountability and change

perspectives.description: The professors are standing up for free speech rights. If they win, it could set a precedent for how universities must handle protests without violating rights.

Some might argue that the professors involved themselves in a situation that led to their arrests, and that the university had a duty to maintain order. The lawsuit could be seen as a misuse of legal resources.

This lawsuit is part of a wave of legal challenges against universities over protest handling. It reflects a deeper societal debate about the limits of free speech, especially around controversial topics like the Israel-Palestine conflict.
Depending on the outcome, this case could influence how U.S. universities update their protest policies. It may also encourage more faculty to speak out or take legal action in similar situations.
